coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots with intense emission from green to red and their multifunctional applications
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Sai
Cheng, Yinzi
Lin, Chun
Fang, Jiali
Xiang, Weidong
Liang, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: In this report, carbon nanodots (CDs) with tunable full-color emission were successfully synthesized by a solvothermal method. By changing the carbon source (p-phenylenediamine or o-phenylenediamine) and the concentration, green, yellow, orange and red CDs (GCDs, YCDs, OCDs and RCDs) were successfully fabricated. As the concentration of the starting materials was increased, the emission peak was red-shifted. The shifted peaks showed strong intensity and an obvious color change. This concentration-dependent effect was attributed to the reabsorption phenomenon. Carbon source was critical in determining the fluorescence properties and the quantum yield (QY) of full-color CDs was high. The CDs also presented a distinct pH sensitivity. The PL intensity of RCDs showed linear decreases over decreasing pH from 11 to 4 and from 4 to 2. Fluorescent oil carrying CDs was successfully applied for various purposes such as anti-counterfeiting, information encryption and storage purposes. An RCD film was successfully fabricated that could improve the correlated color temperature and color rendering index of white light-emitting diodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescence
Carbon nanodots
Concentration-dependent emission
Full-color emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH adjustments of the obtained CDs
Optical responses of fluorescent CDs to pH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Nature Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51472183 and 51672192 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) A schematic illustration of the synthesis the CDs, starting materials:p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, N,N-dimethyl formamide; (b) The photograph of four samples under UV light.

(a) The TEM image of GCDs, and the inset picture is the corresponding HRTEM image of GCDs; (b) the size distribution of GCDs; (c) the TEM image of RCDs, and the inset in TEM pictures is the HRTEM image; (d) size distribution of RCDs.
(a) FTIR images of GCDs and RCDs; (b) High-resolution XPS full spectra of GCDs, (c) and the corresponding XPS C1s spectrum (d) XPS N 1s spectrum and (e) XPS O 1s spectrum.

The PL spectra of the GCDs (a), YCDs (b) at different excitation wavelengths (recorded from 400 nm to 480 nm in 20 nm increments). And The PL spectra of OCDs (c), RCDs (d) excited by 440 nm–520 nm (recorded in 20 increments).

The UV–vis absorption spectra of GCDs (blue line) and RCDs (red line), the inset image in the top right-hand concern is UV–vis in visible region of GCDs, and the inset image in the bottom right corner is UV–vis in visible region of RCDs.
The fluorescence lifetimes of GCDs, YCDs, OCDs and RCDs.
The PL spectra of RCDs at different pH ranging from 2 to 11. Excitation wavelength are 540 nm, and the inset is linear relationship between PL intensity and pH (from 2 to 11).

(a) The normalized EL spectra for the fabricated LEDs (the black line is EL spectra of pure PiG, and the red line is EL spectra of PiG/CF) and (b) color coordinates of the LEDs (the top dot is the PiG and the bottom dot is PiG/CF).
Synthesis of CDs by different combinations of C source and their color.
XPS elemental analysis results of the GCDs and RCDs.
The lifetime and QY of GCDs, YCDs, OCDs and RCDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots with intense emission from green to red and their multifunctional applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou, 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this report, carbon nanodots (CDs) with tunable full-color emission were successfully synthesized by a solvothermal method. By changing the carbon source (p-phenylenediamine or o-phenylenediamine) and the concentration, green, yellow, orange and red CDs (GCDs, YCDs, OCDs and RCDs) were successfully fabricated. As the concentration of the starting materials was increased, the emission peak was red-shifted. The shifted peaks showed strong intensity and an obvious color change. This concentration-dependent effect was attributed to the reabsorption phenomenon. Carbon source was critical in determining the fluorescence properties and the quantum yield (QY) of full-color CDs was high. The CDs also presented a distinct pH sensitivity. The PL intensity of RCDs showed linear decreases over decreasing pH from 11 to 4 and from 4 to 2. Fluorescent oil carrying CDs was successfully applied for various purposes such as anti-counterfeiting, information encryption and storage purposes. An RCD film was successfully fabricated that could improve the correlated color temperature and color rendering index of white light-emitting diodes.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbonaceous graphene quantum dots (QDs) or carbon nanodots (CDs) have been extensively studied by different groups such as graphene QD, carbon nanodots (CDs) [1–4]. As a new photoluminescent (PL) materials, CDs have attracted intense interest, because they offer the virtues of such as excellent biocompatibility, unique optical features and nontoxicity [5,6]. CDs can be prepared by various methods using top-down or bottom-up carbonization. The applicability of CDs in fields such as fluorescent probes and sensors, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), and bioimaging has been demonstrated [7–10]. Many CDs synthesized in the last decade exhibit only short-wavelength emissions. Although many reports have demonstrated shifts in CD emission wavelengths with changes in the excitation wavelength, the dominant peaks have remained in the short-wavelength range, and the shifted peaks showed much weaker intensities than the dominant peaks; thus, they remain classified as short-wavelength CDs [11,12]. This means that these CDs were still short-wavelength CDs. The acquisition of long-wavelength emission and multicolor (capable of single-wavelength excitation) emissive CDs remains challenging. This impedes their further applications of CDs, particularly in the field of biomedicine and optoelectronic devices, because short-wavelength CDs may experience interference by the intrinsic PL of the cells; meanwhile, long-wavelength CDs based phosphors are indispensable components for fabricating phosphor-converted LEDs (pc-LEDs) [11,14]. Acquiring long-wavelength CDs remains necessary and important. Some recent reports have demonstrated methods to acquire multicolor CDs under one single excitation wavelengths by adjusting the reagent (different acid-base solutions), carbon source and synthesis conditions (reaction temperature and time) [11–14]. They believed the PL properties were related to different sizes and/or different surface state of CDs. However, few have concentration-dependent CDs emission changes [15,16].

Solution pH strongly affect biochemical and physiological actions and processes and changes thereof may induce various diseases. Thus, the accurate measurement of pH values is important in widespread applications, including biology, electronic devices, and medical diagnostics [17–19]. The various advantages of CDs have demonstrated their suitability in pH sensing. Unfortunately, most reported CDs for the pH sensing show short-wavelength emission which is unsuitable for biological application because short-wavelength excitation lights can damage living cells or biosystems [20–22]. Meanwhile, white LEDs (WLEDs) are the next-generation lighting devices of choice because they have long operational lifetimes, compact designs, and remarkable energy savings. For indoor lighting, a color rendering index (CRI) exceeding 80, correlated color temperature (CCT) below 4000 K, excellent spectral overlap with eye sensitivity functions, and a warm white shade are required. However, efficient long-wavelength CDs are scarce. Only by QD-like design to fully tune PL can CDs be successfully applied in most applications.

In this study, green, yellow, orange, and red CDs (GCDs, YCDs, OCDs, and RCDs, respectively) were successfully fabricated by changing the carbon source and the concentration of starting materials. YCDs, OCDs and RCDs were simultaneously excited by one excitation wavelength and three samples showed independence from excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, GCDs showed small PL shifts with varying excitation wavelengths. Structural and spectroscopic characterizations revealed that p-phenylenediamine could be much better than o-phenylenediamine in forming long-wavelength CDs. Moreover, these CDs can be used for pH sensing, fluorescent oils and LEDs.

p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF chromatographic grade), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and nitric acid (HNO3) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All of the chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade (expect DMF) and used as received without further purification.

CDs with tunable concentration-dependent full-color emission were synthesized by a solvothermal process. In a typical procedure, the appropriate p-phenylenediamine or o-phenylenediamine (0.005 g or 0.05 g) precursor was dissolved uniformly in 10 mL DMF. The transparent reddish solution was transferred to a 20-mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Next, the mixture was placed in an oven and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After 4 h, the solution color changed to deep purple-red, indicating the generation of the CDs. The detailed process is noted in Table 1 . It is worth noting that we increased concentration of carbon source before treatment for the different samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH adjustments of the obtained CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Solvents of different pH (HNO3 and NaOH) were added to the RCDs solvent and the mixtures were equilibrated for 15 min. The PL emission spectra were then collected at the excitation wavelength of 520 nm.

To investigate the applicability of the RCDs as red phosphors in warm WLEDs, a color converter with an RCD film (RCF) stacked on a yellow-emitting phosphor-in-glass (PiG) plate (PiG/RCF) was fabricated. The PiG plate was fabricated by the low-temperature co-sintering of a Ce:YAG yellow phosphor and TeO2-ZnO-Sb2O3-Al2O3-B2O3-Na2O glass, as reported previously by our group. The RCF was prepared by mixing 0.2 mg RCDs with 1.5 g polyvinyl butyral (PVB) in 10 mL ethanol with sonication to eliminate bubbles. Next, screen-printing technology was adopted to deposit the RCD-embedded PVB film on the PiG, which was then dried at room temperature (25 °C). Finally, LEDs were constructed by coupling the as-fabricated bi-layered color converter with a GaN chip.

The microstructures of the samples observed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a FEI Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Axi Ultra DLD spectrometer with monochromatic Al Kα radiation as the excitation source. Fourier-transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using a Bruker Equinox 55 FTIR spectrometer from 500 to 4000 cm−1. Absorption spectra were acquired with an ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Mode V-570, JASCO). PL and PL quantum yields (QYs) were acquired using a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluromax-4P spectrophotometer equipped with an absolute QY measurement apparatus. Two 10 mm fluorescence cuvettes were separately used to fill equal volume of CDs solution and pure solvent as contrast. The fluorescence spectra of CDs and pure solvent were recorded. Finally, fluorescence software was used to calculate the QY according to the spectra of all sample and pure solvent. Subsequently, CDs solution was placed in the integrating sphere and excited with monochromatic light at the optimal excitation wavelength. Time-resolved PL lifetime measurements were performed using a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) lifetime spectroscopy system with a picosecond-pulsed diode laser (EPL-400 nm) as the single wavelength excitation light source. Optical properties such as color rendering index (CRI), color coordinates (CIE), correlated color temperature (CCT) were evaluated employing an integrating sphere (PMS-50, Everfine, China) under a forward current of 20 mA.

Here, CDs with concentration-dependent emission were acquired by the reaction of phenylenediamine with DMF. Schematics depicting the synthesis of four CDs types are shown in Fig. 1 a. Under UV light irradiation, the four samples exhibit bright green, yellow, orange, and red fluorescence respectively, as seen in Fig. 1b. From Table 1, increases in the concentration of the carbon source induce shifts in PL from green to yellow and from orange to red. In this study, among the different concentrations and carbon sources, we discuss GCDs and RCDs as typical samples in the following section.

The morphologies and sizes of the GCDs and RCDs were characterized by the TEM. As revealed in Fig. 2 , the two CDs are both mostly uniform spherical nanoparticles with the average sizes of ∼5.5 nm (GCDs) and ∼6.5 nm (RCDs). The HRTEM images inset in Fig. 2a and c show well-resolved lattice fringes with the interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) lattice of graphite along the [001] direction [11,14,23]. Meanwhile, for determination of lattice parameters by single lattice planes more authentic, the fast Fourier transformation (FFT) images were exhibited in Fig. S1, the result is corresponded with the HRTEM. The different sizes of the GCDs and RCDs clearly demonstrate that the PL shift is related quantum size effects [24,25].

Knowing the surface groups on CDs can assist in understanding their luminescence mechanisms. Therefore, the surface functional groups of the GCDs and RCDs were identified by FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. 3 a. Many bonds are observed in the CDs including OH/NH (3535 cm−1), CH (2934 cm−1 and 2862 cm−1), CN (1447 cm−1) and aromatic ether (1245 cm−1) [26,27]. The obtained CDs contain aromatic structures with abundant surface NH2 groups. Meanwhile the hydrophilic groups such as OH, NH endow the CDs with excellent solubility in various solvents, such as water, DMF, and ethanol. To obtain further insight into the CD components, XPS survey spectra were obtained. Fig. 3b exhibits the full XPS spectrum of the GCDs. Three typical peaks appear: C 1s (283 eV), N 1s (399 eV) and O 1s (533 eV). In the high-resolution spectrum of the C 1s peak (Fig. 3c), the peak is deconvoluted into two peaks located at ∼284.6 eV and ∼285.5 eV, corresponding to CC/CC and CN/CO respectively. The N 1s fitting peaks (Fig. 3d) at ∼398.4 eV and ∼399.1 eV arise from pyridinic N and amino N, respectively. The O 1s fitting peak shown in Fig. 3e contain peaks at ∼531.9 eV and ∼533.7 eV originating from CO and CO [28,29]. These indicate that the CDs contain many nitrogenous fused-ring structures. Meanwhile, the XPS spectra of the RCDs were investigated and are depicted in Fig. S2. The atomic percentages of C in the GCDs and RCDs are noted in Table 2 . Obviously, the N content of RCDs is higher than that of GCDs. Although the mechanism for the highly efficient red emissions from the CDs is poorly investigated. Researchers have proposed that large particles and high N contents contribute to efficient long-wavelength emission [11,30].

PL characteristics are among the most important features of CDs, endowing utility in fluorescent sensors, imaging, and light-emitting devices. As depicted in Fig. 4 , the four CDs show emission peaks at 520 nm, 550 nm, 575 nm and 608 nm. The YCDs, OCDs and RCDs exhibit excitation-independent emission feature, while GCDs demonstrate small PL shifts with varying excitation wavelengths. By increasing the concentration of the starting materials, longer-wavelength are obtained CDs. Uniform dispersion of the CDs in solvents at high concentrations could be unstable because of the extremely short distances between CDs and the high surface energies of the CDs. As with the crystallization of an oversaturated solution, the dendritic aggregation (one method for quickly decreasing surface energy) of a few CDs can reduce the supersaturation degree. A possible schematic is shown in Fig. S3. Reabsorption phenomena are easily observed for sufficiently high contents of CDs in solution [20,21]. Eventually, the high concentration of CDs in the solution will cause a red-shift in emissivity. When the concentration of p-phenylenediamine is increased to 0.1 g, the obtained CDs show a dark red color caused by serious aggregation; simultaneously, fluorescence suffers severe quenching. However, when the concentration of o-phenylenediamine is increased to 0.1 g, the PL continues to exhibit a yellow color, confirming that the red fluorescence is closely linked to the starting materials (as depicted in Fig. S4). Therefore, the carbon sources is critical in forming CDs and determining the CD properties. After careful testing, including TEM, XPS and FTIR analysis, only the particle sizes and N contents of the GCDs and RCDs were found differ distinctly. Therefore, conjugate effects or quantum-size effects [23] and higher N contents may be at least partly responsible for the red emission of the RCDs, but deeper analysis remains necessary to address this issue.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the GCDs and RCDs are depicted in Fig. 5 , showing that the carbon source can also affect the absorption properties. GCDs have a broad absorption even extending to the visible region, including two peaks at 240 nm and 298 nm in the UV region and a peak in the visible region. The peaks at 240 nm and 298 nm are ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp
2 domain and CN respectively [22,31,32]. The spectrum of RCDs presents a red-shift phenomenon in both the lower- and higher-energy absorption bands. Compared to GCDs, the absorption bands in the UV region of RCDs shift from 240 nm and 298 nm–250 nm and 318 nm respectively, while higher-energy absorption band shifts from 450 nm to 520 nm. The peak at 520 nm is attributed to Mie scattering caused by RCDs, representing the typical absorption of an aromatic system and indicating extended conjugation in the CDs structure [33]. These red-shifts in the electronic absorption transitions demonstrate that the RCDs contain smaller electronic band gaps than the GCDs do, which correspond to the longer emission of the RCDs.

The QY of the GCDs, YCDs, OCDs and RCDs is measured as ∼56%, 58%, 52%, and 30% respectively. QY is related to number of photons incident on the sample volume (α) and number of photons emitted by the sample (ε) [34].

It is interesting to find that the QY shows dependence on the CDs concentration. This correlation is not accidental and agrees with reports in previous studies [35,36]. Zhang et al. [33] proposed that the QY of CDs was related to the number of molecular fluorophores: more molecular fluorophores correspond to greater photon emitting which cause higher QY of the CDs. Both phenylenediamine and DMF are non-fluorescent small molecules. Firstly, the amino groups and benzene rings of phenylenediamine and the hydroxyl groups of DMF participate in a complex condensation reaction including intermolecular condensation between phenylenediamine molecules themselves and between phenylenediamine molecules and DMF molecules to remove water molecules and form small fluorescent molecules [37–39]. With increasing concentration, the condensation degree gradually increases, and the small fluorescent molecules gradually grow up. With the further increases in concentration, the condensation degree also increases, as well as the number of molecular fluorophores increases. Finally the number of photons emitted increases, the QY also increased. In other words, the low concentration of CDs exhibit low photos emitting which eventually lead to a low QY. However, high concentrations also caused aggregation and the PL is thus greatly quenched because of the excessive resonance energy transfer or π-π interaction, similar to the behavior of organic dyes [40–43]. Fluorescence decay is a one of the intrinsic characteristic of an emitting-state. Therefore, different emitting states exhibit different decay behaviors. GCDs and RCDs are very different, whether considering the raw materials or the fluorescence properties. Hence, the two CDs types also have different behaviors, as presented in Fig. 6 . The fitted PL decay curves are provided according to the following double exponential function [44]:
(1)
Y
( t )
=
α 1
exp
( - τ /
t 1
)
+
α 2
exp
( - τ /
t 2
)
wherein α
1 and α
2 represent the fractional contributions of decay lifetimes t
1 and t
2 . The average lifetime can be deduced using the relation [44]:
(2)
α
a v
=
(
α 1
τ 1 2
+
α 2
τ 2 2
)
/
(
α 1
τ 1
+
α 2
τ 2
)

The emission decays of the two CDs contain the two processes of the short- and long-lived components t
1 and t
2 , respectively. The fitting parameters are summarized in Table 3 : the GCDs exhibit ultrashort lifetimes, while RCDs show longer lifetimes. The CDs lifetimes are also dependent on the concentrations, which are slightly decreased when the doped carbon source concentration is increased. We estimate the radiative and nonradiative decay rates of CDs with different concentrations using Eq. (3) [45,46]:
(3)
1 τ
=
K r
+
K
n r
where Kr and Knr are the radiative and non-radiative decay rates respectively, and τ is the average lifetime. The evident surface interactions of CDs among reactive intermediates, cause the strengthening of Knr as the concentration is increased from 0.005 g to 0.05 g meaning that the surface interactions among reactive intermediates promote non-radiative decay processes [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical responses of fluorescent CDs to pH
ce:section.ce:para: Interesting, the stability of the four CDs types at different pH values was investigated, surprisingly strong optical responses to changes in pH were observed. In this study, we selected RCDs for detailed investigated. A series of fluorescent RCD solutions with pH ranging systematically from 2 to 11 were mixed and the PL intensities of the RCDs in solutions of different pH values were monitored with fluorescence spectrometry. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , the PL intensity is improved rapidly when the pH is changed from 2 to 4 with the linear correlation coefficient of 0.996 (Fig. 7). The rate of increase decreases for pH increasing from 4 to 11 with the linear correlation coefficient of 0.991 (Fig. 7). Introducing a strongly alkaline solution such as NaOH solution into the H+-quenched RCD solution causes the RCDs to recover fluorescence, indicating that detection of pH using RCDs is reversible. Considering that long-wavelength emissions are necessary for biomedical imaging and detection, the as-synthesized RCDs represent good candidates for biological pH sensing.

Based on the remarkable PL properties of the CDs, they can be used as a fluorescent oils for various purposes such as anti-counterfeiting, information encryption and information storage. Fig. S4 presents a fluorescent printing paper cut to contain a flower and leaves. The paper was soaked in advance with a dispersion of CDs under UV light. The stability of the fluorescent printing paper was evaluated by monitoring its PL spectrum under 365 nm light irradiation. After 1 months, the flower and leaves still show bright fluorescence, suggesting a high stability (Fig. S5).

As a proof-of-concept experiment, a Ce: YAG PiG coated with RCF was introduced to act as a color converter for warm WLED by coupling with a GaN chip. As demonstrated in Fig. 8 a, the electroluminescence (EL) spectra of the constructed LEDs, present an emission band peaking at 460 nm from the GaN chip and a broad yellow emission band from PiG. Unlike the pure PiG (black line), the PiG/RCF (red line) exhibits an obvious red emission band attributed to the RCDs. As exhibited in Fig. 8b, the CIE color coordinates are shifted from (0.35, 0.39) to (0.42, 0.40), the CCT is decreased from 4865 K to 3222 K, and the CRI is increased from 69 to 86. These results demonstrate that the CDs have potential applicability in in-door WLEDs.

CDs with continuously tunable emissions were successfully synthesized by an easy solvothermal methods. Red shifts in emission were observed when the carbon source and its concentration were changed. This concentration-dependent PL arose from the resorption phenomenon. We propose that the precursor of p-phenylenediamine is critical in forming the unique structure of long-wavelength CDs unique structure. The QY of GCDs, YCDs, OCDs and RCDs were 56%, 58%, 52% and 40% respectively. In addition, the PL intensity of RCDs varied alone was varied with change in the pH showing good linear relationships with pH changes from 11 to 4 and from 4 to 2 as well as excellent PL reversibility. Fluorescent oil bearing the CDs was successfully applied. Finally, we further prepared PiG/RCF to realize a warm LED (CIE:(0.42,0.40),CCT:3222 K, CRI:86). Therefore, the as-synthesized CDs demonstrate good candidacy for multifunctional applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51472183 and 51672192).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.01.194.
